name: Baghdad Battery Theory
description: The Baghdad Battery Theory posits that ceramic jars discovered near Baghdad, Iraq, dating back to the Parthian (247 BCE – 224 CE) or Sasanian (224–650 CE) periods, were used as galvanic cells to produce electricity. Proponents suggest these 'batteries' powered electroplating, pain relief, or religious rituals. The theory gained traction in the 20th century, fueled by popular media and alternative history circles. The 'battery' consists of a clay jar containing a copper cylinder, an iron rod, and traces of acidic substances like vinegar or wine. While some experiments have shown that these jars can produce a small voltage, the mainstream archaeological community largely dismisses the battery theory. They argue there's no definitive evidence of electrical usage in ancient Mesopotamia, such as electroplated artifacts or electrical wiring. Alternative explanations for the jars include storage vessels for scrolls or other items. The iron rod may have been used to secure a scroll inside the copper cylinder, preventing damage during transport. The presence of acidic substances could be attributed to the jar's original contents decomposing over time. The theory, despite its appeal, lacks concrete archaeological support and relies heavily on speculation and circumstantial evidence.
subtitle: Ancient Mesopotamian Electric Batteries?

claims.claim: The Baghdad jars were used to generate electricity.
claims.evidence: The jars contain components similar to a battery: a copper cylinder, an iron rod, and a container.
claims.criticism: No electrical devices or evidence of electroplating have been found in Mesopotamia dating to that period. The voltage produced is very low and impractical for most uses.

claims.claim: The generated electricity was used for electroplating objects with gold or silver.
claims.evidence: Some proponents argue that electroplating was known in ancient times and the jars were used for this purpose.
claims.criticism: There is no archaeological evidence of electroplated artifacts from Mesopotamia that predate the common era. The process of electroplating requires a more sophisticated understanding of chemistry and electricity than is believed to have existed at the time.

claims.claim: The batteries were used for pain relief through electrotherapy.
claims.evidence: Some sources suggest that the jars could have been used for therapeutic purposes, delivering small electric shocks.
claims.criticism: While rudimentary electrotherapy has been practiced, there's no documented evidence of its use in Mesopotamia or any supporting artifacts. The voltage produced is likely too low for effective pain relief.

keyFigures.name: Wilhelm König
keyFigures.role: Director of the National Museum of Iraq who first proposed the battery theory.

keyFigures.name: Paul Craddock
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who has critiqued the battery theory, arguing for alternative explanations.

mythologyConnections: None directly, but could be linked to myths about divine power or magical objects if interpreted as a source of supernatural energy.

timeline.event: Wilhelm König discovers the Baghdad jars and proposes the battery theory.
König publishes a paper detailing his theory about the jars being ancient batteries.
Experiments are conducted to test the battery theory, demonstrating that the jars can produce a small voltage.

sources.author: Paul Craddock
sources.work: Gilding of Metals in the Old World
sources.title: Paul Craddock
